planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist to consistently wet soil in partial to full shade; excellent for rain gardens and wet areas under trees.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic soil in full sun to full shade; Virginia sweetspire is one of the most adaptable native shrubs, performing in conditions ranging from dry shade to standing water and from full sun to deep shade, while providing three seasons of interest. The pendulous white flower racemes in June–July are fragrant and attract many bee species; the foliage turns brilliant red, orange, and purple in fall, often holding color well into December in the South. The shrub spreads by stolons to form colonies. Prune in early spring to remove oldest canes at the base; avoid heavy pruning as it blooms on old wood. The 'Henry's Garnet' cultivar has particularly vivid fall color.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall; note that seedling-grown plants have variable fragrance — purchase named cultivars or divisions from plants whose fragrance you have evaluated in person.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Carolina allspice is a native understory shrub of the Southeast with extraordinary, deeply fragrant maroon flowers in late spring that smell like strawberry, banana, and clove combined. The flowers are pollinated by small beetles that become temporarily trapped while feeding, ensuring cross-pollination. Fragrance intensity varies tremendously among seed-grown plants — evaluate or buy named cultivars like 'Athens' (fragrant, cream-colored) for reliable results. The bark and leaves are also aromatic. The shrub spreads by root suckers to form a colony over time; remove suckers to maintain a single-stemmed specimen or allow to form a thicket.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in poor, sandy, or gravelly soil in full sun; scarify seed by nicking or soaking in hot water for 24 hours before sowing to break dormancy. Self-sows reliably to return each year.

directions: Scarify and direct sow after last frost in lean, sandy, or disturbed soil in full sun; do not add fertilizer or enrich the soil, as partridge pea is a nitrogen-fixer that thrives in poor conditions where competitors cannot crowd it out. The bright yellow flowers in midsummer are the primary pollen source for bumble bee specialist Xylocopa and Megachile species that cannot use other pollen sources. It is also a critical larval host plant for the cloudless sulphur, sleepy orange, and little yellow butterflies. The seed pods provide food for quail, wild turkey, and bobwhite. The plant behaves as a self-sowing annual and colonizes disturbed areas, roadside shoulders, and restoration plantings rapidly.

planting.note: Surface sow seeds 4–6 weeks before last frost (cold stratification for 30 days improves germination) or divide established clumps in spring.

directions: Surface sow seed in moist, average to rich soil in full sun to partial shade; ironweed produces some of the most intensely violet-purple flowers in the late-summer and fall native garden (late August through October), providing critical nectar for migrating monarchs and native bees. Its strong, wiry stems (hence 'ironweed') resist lodging and require no staking. The plant spreads by self-seeding and root division; remove unwanted seedlings in spring when small. Pinch the growing tips once in late spring to encourage a shorter, more branched plant. Excellent when combined with tall goldenrods and asters for a spectacular native fall display.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in containers of heavy clay soil and submerge in 4–12 inches of water after water temperatures reach 60°F; or plant directly in shallow pond margins.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in shallow water (4–12 inches deep) in clay-loam soil in full sun; pickerel rush is one of the most essential native aquatic plants for pond margins, rain gardens, and wetland restoration, providing blue-violet flower spikes from June through October that are heavily used by bumblebees and long-tongued native bees. The glossy, arrow-shaped leaves provide cover for frogs, herons hunt at the plant's base, and waterfowl eat the seeds. Remove spent flower stalks to encourage rebloom. Divide crowded colonies every 3–4 years in spring, keeping each division attached to a healthy rhizome section.
